content:
Design patterns are not interview decoration. They are the invisible habits that keep real codebases from turning into expensive confusion.

11 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most developers do not hate design patterns.
They hate the way design patterns are taught.
Too abstract.
Too academic.
Too disconnected from the bugs they are actually fixing at 1:30 AM.
I have seen teams laugh at “design patterns” while accidentally reinventing them in controllers, services, React components, queues, payment flows, database layers, and authentication systems. They did not call it Strategy, Factory, Adapter, Observer, or Singleton. They called it “that helper,” “that wrapper,” “that config file,” or “that event thing.”
The name was not the problem.
The lack of judgment was.
A design pattern is not a trophy for sounding smart. It is a reusable solution to a problem that keeps coming back. Used well, it reduces surprise. Used badly, it becomes ceremony with better vocabulary.
The painful truth is this: most developers already use design patterns every day. They just do it accidentally…
